source_paper_bibtex: @article{Ahmadi_2026, title={Generalized Ellipsoids}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0643}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0643}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Ahmadi, Amir Ali and Chaudhry, Abraar and Dibek, Cemil}, year={2026}, month=jan }
context:
Section 7 (Future Research Directions), page 31 (PDF pagination in the provided text)

A related question is: how well can one approximate an \(n\)-dimensional symmetric convex body with a finite intersection of \(m\) (co-centered) ellipsoids, with \(m\) growing potentially with \(n\)? Since we have shown that a GE-\(d\), with \(d = 2m-3\), can exactly represent an intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids (see Corollary 5.6 and Theorem 5.1), progress on this question can potentially improve our approximation factor in Theorem 5.7.
title: Approximate symmetric convex bodies by intersections of co-centered ellipsoids in high dimensions
background_latex:
Let \(C\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) be a symmetric convex body, i.e., a compact convex set with nonempty interior such that \(x\in C\Rightarrow -x\in C\). An ellipsoid (centered at the origin) is a set of the form
\[E(Q):=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^TQx\le 1\},\]
where \(Q\in \mathbb{S}^n_{++}\) is symmetric positive definite. (Equivalently, an ellipsoid is the unit ball of a quadratic norm.)

For an integer \(m\ge 1\), an intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids means a set of the form
\[\bigcap_{i=1}^m E(Q_i)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^TQ_i x\le 1\ \text{for all } i=1,\dots,m\},\]
with all ellipsoids centered at the origin.

Given two symmetric convex bodies \(K,L\subset \mathbb{R}^n\), a standard multiplicative (sandwich) approximation notion is: \(K\) \(\varepsilon\)-approximates \(L\) if
\[K\subseteq L\subseteq (1+\varepsilon)K,\]
where \((1+\varepsilon)K:=\{(1+\varepsilon)x: x\in K\}\) is a uniform radial scaling.

The paper introduces generalized ellipsoids (GEs) and shows (i) any intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids can be represented exactly as a generalized ellipsoid of degree \(d\le 2m-3\), and (ii) every symmetric convex body can be approximated by a GE with a quantitative degree bound obtained via known results on polytopic approximation. The question below asks directly about approximation by intersections of ellipsoids, which would in turn sharpen the degree-vs-accuracy bounds for GE approximation.
problem_definition_latex:
For fixed dimension \(n\) and approximation parameter \(\varepsilon>0\), determine (or tightly bound) the smallest integer \(m=m(n,\varepsilon)\) such that for every symmetric convex body \(C\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) there exist positive definite matrices \(Q_1,\dots,Q_m\in\mathbb{S}^n_{++}\) with
\[\bigcap_{i=1}^m \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^TQ_i x\le 1\}\ \subseteq\ C\ \subseteq\ (1+\varepsilon)\,\bigcap_{i=1}^m \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^TQ_i x\le 1\}.
\]
Equivalently, characterize the best possible approximation quality achievable by intersections of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids as a function of \(m\) and \(n\).

location_in_paper: Section 7 (Future Research Directions), page 31 (PDF pagination in the provided text)
open_problem_quote: A related question is: how well can one approximate an \(n\)-dimensional symmetric convex body with a finite intersection of \(m\) (co-centered) ellipsoids, with \(m\) growing potentially with \(n\)? Since we have shown that a GE-\(d\), with \(d = 2m-3\), can exactly represent an intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids (see Corollary 5.6 and Theorem 5.1), progress on this question can potentially improve our approximation factor in Theorem 5.7.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is an extremal, worst-case approximation problem over all symmetric convex bodies in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), so a single finite counterexample search is not well-posed without first positing a specific conjectured bound. While one could search over families (e.g., polytopes) numerically, verifying universal optimality is not feasible by brute-force instance generation.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate approximations by choosing \(m\) matrices \(Q_i\succ 0\) and optimize a computable proxy objective (e.g., minimize the smallest \(\alpha\) such that \(C\subseteq \alpha\cap_i E(Q_i)\) for a fixed target body \(C\), or maximize volume of \(\cap_i E(Q_i)\subseteq C\)). This enables iterative computational search for good constructions and bounds for specific families of bodies, but it does not directly resolve the worst-case theoretical dependence of \(m\) on \((n,\varepsilon)\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for the optimal tradeoff between the number \(m\) of co-centered ellipsoids and the approximation factor \(1+\varepsilon\) for arbitrary symmetric convex bodies \(C\subset\mathbb{R}^n\). For \(m=1\), John’s theorem gives a universal guarantee \(E\subseteq C\subseteq \sqrt{n}E\), and this is essentially tight in worst case, so any \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximation with small \(\varepsilon\) requires \(m\to\infty\) as \(\varepsilon\downarrow 0\) (for fixed \(n\)). The paper "Generalized Ellipsoids" shows that intersections of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids are exactly generalized ellipsoids of degree \(d\le 2m-3\), so bounding \(m(n,\varepsilon)\) would immediately sharpen degree-vs-accuracy bounds for GE approximations.

At present, the only general quantitative \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximation guarantee in the GE paper proceeds indirectly: approximate the polar body \(C^\circ\) by a polytope with \(N\) vertices using Barvinok’s thrifty approximation results, then dualize to obtain a polytope with \(N\) facets approximating \(C\), and finally represent that polytope (or an intersection of semiellipsoids encoding its facets) as a GE with degree \(O(N)\). This yields a GE degree bound of order \(d\lesssim 2(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon}}\log\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon})^n\), but it does not translate into a comparably sharp bound on \(m\) for intersections of ellipsoids because the representation result is one-way: ellipsoid intersections \(\Rightarrow\) GE, not conversely.

Thus the core gap remains: develop direct approximation theory for \(C\) by \(\bigcap_{i=1}^m E(Q_i)\) with explicit dependence of \(m\) on \(n\) and \(\varepsilon\), and ideally matching lower bounds. Promising directions include (i) identifying extremal bodies for this approximation model (e.g., \(\ell_1\) or \(\ell_\infty\) balls, zonoids, or bodies with many nearly-flat regions), (ii) relating ellipsoid-intersection approximation to covering numbers/entropy of the unit sphere under the norm \(\|\cdot\|_C\), and (iii) exploiting algorithmic constructions (e.g., iterative selection of supporting ellipsoids) informed by optimization over ellipsoid intersections (as in work on quadratic optimization over such sets). No forward-citation evidence of a solution is available here, so the problem should be regarded as open.

sources.title: Thrifty approximations of convex bodies by polytopes
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives near-optimal bounds on approximating a symmetric convex body by a polytope with controlled number of vertices/facets, yielding rates like \(N\approx (\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon}}\log\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon})^n\) for \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximation in fixed dimension.
sources.connection: The GE paper’s current quantitative approximation bound (Theorem 5.7) reduces approximation by generalized ellipsoids to polytopic approximation via polarity; any improvement in approximating by intersections of ellipsoids would sharpen these bounds beyond what is inherited from polytopes.

sources.title: A Course in Convexity
sources.theorem_or_result: Comprehensive reference on convex bodies, polarity, and approximation by polytopes; includes duality facts used to translate vertex bounds to facet bounds and vice versa.
sources.connection: Provides the convex-geometric toolkit (polarity, support functions, approximation notions) underlying the reduction from approximating \(C\) to approximating \(C^\circ\) by polytopes, which is the route currently used to approximate by generalized ellipsoids and motivates seeking direct ellipsoidal-intersection approximations.

sources.title: Lecture Notes on Modern Convex Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: Standard reference on convex optimization and conic representability, including SOCP/SDP representable sets and operations preserving representability.
sources.connection: Intersections of ellipsoids are convex quadratic representable (hence SOCP/SDP representable). Understanding representability and complexity of such intersections is relevant to algorithmic aspects of searching for good approximations and to comparing GE vs finite ellipsoid intersections.

sources.title: Minimum-Volume Ellipsoids: Theory and Algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops theory/algorithms for minimum-volume enclosing ellipsoids and related ellipsoidal approximations (John/Löwner ellipsoids) with quantitative guarantees.
sources.connection: John/Löwner ellipsoids give the baseline \(\sqrt{n}\) (or \(n\)) factor approximation by a single ellipsoid; the open problem asks how the approximation improves when allowing intersections of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids, so this is a natural starting point and comparison benchmark.

sources.title: Extremum problems with inequalities as subsidiary conditions
sources.theorem_or_result: John’s theorem: every symmetric convex body \(C\subset\mathbb{R}^n\) has a maximum-volume inscribed ellipsoid \(E\) with \(E\subseteq C\subseteq \sqrt{n}E\).
sources.connection: Gives the best-possible universal guarantee for \(m=1\). The open problem seeks the optimal tradeoff \(m(n,\varepsilon)\) for intersections of \(m\) ellipsoids, generalizing John’s single-ellipsoid approximation.

sources.title: On maximization of quadratic form over intersection of ellipsoids with common center
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes SDP relaxations and approximation ratios for optimizing quadratic forms over intersections of co-centered ellipsoids; provides bounds depending on the number of ellipsoids.
sources.connection: While not directly about approximating arbitrary convex bodies by ellipsoid intersections, it develops quantitative understanding of geometry/optimization over such intersections and how complexity scales with \(m\), which is relevant for algorithmic approaches to constructing approximations.

sources.title: Generalized Ellipsoids
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces generalized ellipsoids (GE-d) and proves: (i) any intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids is exactly a GE-d with \(d\le 2m-3\); (ii) any symmetric convex body can be \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximated by a GE-d with \(d\) bounded via polytopic approximation.
sources.connection: This is the source paper formulating the open problem: improving approximation of symmetric convex bodies by intersections of co-centered ellipsoids would sharpen the degree-vs-accuracy bounds for GE approximation and clarify the expressive power of finite ellipsoid intersections.
